column21:
As a marine biologist, I am fortunate to spend a lot of time underwater - often witnessing incredible beauty, but also the harsh reality of human impact, such as plastic pollution. I want to be able to capture both: the awe-inspiring biodiversity and the “ugliness” that threatens it, and turn these moments into powerful visuals that can educate, move, and inspire people who may never see the underwater world themselves.

I previously conducted research in the Azores, and its rich biodiversity and dramatic seascapes left a lasting impression on me. It feels like the perfect setting to learn how to translate science and emotion into compelling underwater imagery. BTM Academy stood out to me because it combines professional-level training with real-world ocean experiences. I see it as the ideal place to bridge my scientific background with creative storytelling and to begin building the skills I need to communicate ocean issues through film.
column22:
My ultimate vision is to integrate underwater filming as a core, complementary part of my career as a marine biologist. I believe that images and films can reach people on an emotional level in a way that scientific papers alone often cannot. By combining rigorous science with strong visuals, I want to help make the ocean “real” for those who have never looked beneath the surface.

Even though I am only at the beginning of my filming journey, I dream of one day contributing to impactful documentaries focused on shark and ray conservation and on the reality of microplastic pollution in our oceans. I hope to create stories that not only inform but also empower viewers to care, change behavior, and support conservation efforts. In the long term, I would like my work to sit at the intersection of research, storytelling, and advocacy - using underwater filming as a tool to protect marine ecosystems and the species I work with.
column23:
From this internship, I hope to gain a solid foundation in the full process of underwater storytelling - from capturing images to crafting a narrative. This includes learning how to handle cameras and equipment underwater, how to compose and light shots in a challenging environment, and how to edit footage into a cohesive, emotionally engaging story. 

I also want to understand the technical side in depth: gear maintenance, dive logistics for filming, workflow for post-production, and best practices for working ethically with wildlife. Beyond the hard skills, I hope to meet and learn from people who share my passion for the ocean and visual storytelling. Building a network of mentors and peers in underwater filmmaking would be incredibly valuable as I continue to develop my own voice and carve out a path that connects marine science and film.
column24:
Although my experience in (underwater) photography and filming is still limited, my commitment to the ocean runs very deep. I have made it my life’s mission to protect marine ecosystems, and this mission drives me to work hard, to learn quickly, and to push myself outside my comfort zone when it is in service of the ocean.

I am very comfortable in the water, with extensive experience diving and conducting research at sea. My background as a marine biologist gives me a strong understanding of marine life, animal behavior, and ecosystems, which can be an invaluable asset when planning and capturing meaningful underwater stories. I also already engage in science communication and education, so I understand how to translate complex topics into accessible messages.

What I may lack in technical filmmaking experience, I make up for in motivation, resilience, and a genuine passion for using storytelling to protect the ocean. I would bring curiosity, a strong work ethic, and a conservation-focused perspective to the BTM Academy team, and I am ready to fully commit to making the most of this opportunity.

column21:
I have always been drawn to the underwater world through science, fieldwork and image-making.
With a background in marine biology and a strong practice in underwater photography, I developed a deep interest in visual storytelling below the surface.
Over time, I became increasingly interested in underwater filmmaking as a complementary medium, allowing me to explore movement, behavior and atmosphere, while continuing to refine my photographic approach.
I chose BTM Academy because of its hands-on, mentorship-based approach, where photography and filmmaking are both considered essential tools for telling meaningful underwater stories.
The philosophy of BTM, combining creativity, technical skills and respect for the marine environment, strongly resonates with my desire to grow as a visual storyteller, both in photography and video.
column22:
My long-term vision is to build a solid path in underwater imagery, combining photography and film in the service of strong, ocean-related stories. I aim to develop visual projects that portray the marine world with sensitivity and depth, finding a balance between an artistic approach to imagery and a deep understanding of the living world.

My goal is, over time, to create underwater wildlife documentaries conceived as immersive works, allowing space for observation and storytelling. I want to work on long-term projects focused on behavior, interactions and the raw beauty of the underwater world, using a visual language inspired by major wildlife documentaries.

Through these projects, I primarily seek to convey emotion and a sense of wonder for the living world, convinced that the beauty and poetry of the underwater realm are, in themselves, powerful drivers of awareness.

In this perspective, I am also eager to discover and understand teamwork, the demands of professional production, and the realities of working in the field.
column23:
Through this internship, I hope to gain a concrete and formative experience in the field of underwater imagery. I want to improve my technical skills in both photography and filmmaking, particularly in terms of camera work, camera movement, light reading and the overall visual coherence of a project.

I also see this internship as a rich field experience, allowing me to work in real conditions, spend time in the water, observe marine life and adapt to the natural and logistical constraints specific to the marine environment.

I would like to learn how to conceive and develop underwater visual projects in a more professional way, from the initial intent to the final outcome, by understanding the different stages of preparation, filming and post-production.

Beyond technical aspects, I want to discover how a production team operates, learn to work within a demanding collaborative environment, and benefit from mentorship and feedback from experienced professionals. This internship represents a key step for me to gain rigor, confidence and clarity in my underwater visual journey.
column24:
I believe I am a strong candidate for this internship because I combine solid field experience in marine environments with a committed practice of underwater imagery. My background in marine biology, along with my experience as a lifeguard and marine naturalist guide, has allowed me to develop a strong understanding of ecosystems, animal behavior and environmental conditions, as well as a high level of comfort in the water.

I am also a certified professional diver (CAH IB), which enables me to operate safely and efficiently underwater, including in demanding conditions.

In parallel, I have been developing personal projects in underwater photography and imagery for several years, with an autonomous, serious and constantly evolving approach. I am used to learning in the field, observing carefully, adapting to situations and critically questioning my own work.

I am a motivated, committed and curious person, with a strong ability to work as part of a team and to integrate into demanding environments. This internship represents a major opportunity for learning and growth, and I am ready to fully invest myself with seriousness, rigor and enthusiasm.

column21:
Before I started filming underwater, I was never really interested in shooting on land. After mastering my freediving skills and discovering the beauty of modeling underwater — both in freediving and scuba modes — I realized that I wanted to move to the other side of the camera and become an underwater photographer.

I’m grateful that I had a mentor and a supportive community back in my home country, Ukraine, who helped me take my first steps and make this dream real. I truly enjoy learning new skills, practicing consistently, and developing them to a professional level. 
Over the past four years, I’ve worked with freediving schools and created personal underwater photoshoots for clients and bloggers.
I’ve always loved having a human presence in my frames, but now I feel ready to explore a new direction where Maureen inhabitant I will be in the first place. As a first step, I completed a marine conservation course and started working with the Atlantis Conservation Program in Dubai, supporting shark and ray release initiatives.
I’m still a student of the ocean. I’m always looking for new expeditions, new challenges, and new mentors — just like when I first started freediving and photography. When I found BTM Academy and the people behind this project, it felt like the right environment for me: a place with the same vision of respecting and protecting the ocean while creating meaningful stories beneath the surface.
column22:
I was born in a seaside town and have always lived close to the sea or the ocean. But I know that many people don’t have this connection. That’s why I believe it’s important to show the beauty of the ocean — and also to show how even people who live far from the sea can still affect it, sometimes harming it from thousands of kilometers away.

Especially today, when we are all connected through social media, we have a powerful opportunity to show this reality directly to people. Even short-form content can make an impact. I truly believe that underwater photographers and videographers can influence awareness about ocean health and help people understand how deeply the ocean matters — not just for those who live near it, but for the entire planet.
column23:
In my professional life, I’ve completely changed my career direction three times. Each time was challenging and sometimes scary, but I actually love that feeling — seeing myself move through uncertainty and grow into a more skilled professional than I was before.

Now, I feel like I’ve found the direction of my professional life that truly fits me and could stay with me long-term. I’m really excited about this internship and hopeful that it will once again change my life in a meaningful way.
column24:
When I look back at who I was before I started scuba diving, freediving, and underwater filmmaking, I sometimes wonder how I ever lived without it. Back then, I was a “land person.” I saw the sea as something that simply existed. Once I began to truly understand the ocean, everything changed. It’s no longer just a place for me; it’s a world I want to keep exploring, learning from, and respecting. I can’t imagine my life without diving deeper into this connection.

Now, I feel ready to become a student of the ocean again. I’m looking for a strong mentor and the right environment to grow. I truly believe that the BTM Academy internship has come into my life at the right time — as a chance to learn more, to challenge myself, and to deepen my understanding of the ocean.

column21:
I fell in love with scuba diving and realised that I like those dives the most where I can observe marine life for a long time and can find different ways, angles and moments to capture something. I realised that my underwater content was also very inspiring for others to see on social media, because many within my circle don’t have a lot of touchpoints with the ocean, living in land-locked Switzerland. My young cousin of 13 years old even decided to write his final paper for high school about ocean protection after having been inspired by my content on Instagram, and others told me they took up scuba diving or felt inspired to learn more about the ocean and especially coral reefs. Thinking how with even just my laienhafter content I could already inspire some people, I feel motivated that by getting more professional at underwater filmmaking, photography, storytelling and editing, I can inspire even more people to respect and protect the ocean.

I chose BTM for my journey because you communicate too clearly, powerfully and without finger-pointing. Exactly what I want to do. I have seen a lot of incredible scientific progress and knowledge in marine conservation, but the message often gets stuck between overly scientific terms and overcomplicated and complex messaging. Ocean conservation needs to be simple, powerful and emotional to reach as many people as possible. And what I see from BTM is exactly that, which is why I dearly hope to learn from you.
column22:
I feel called that by bringing the beautiful sides of the underwater world and its importance even to life on land to as many people in Switzerland as possible on one hand, and also showing the increasing threats the ocean is facing on the other. I want to learn how to take stronger footage, learn how to tell a story in a powerful way so that it sticks with people and inspires emotions, and to then also make the most out of the pictures with proper editing.

I have a ton of motivation and determination, and I’ve got to see a lot of fascinating stories. Now I want to become more professional at capturing all the stories I see around me, to amplify my impact in ocean conservation and contribute to making marine science and marine conservation get more and more into the mainstream. I hope to also reach people who didn’t have a lot of touchpoints with the ocean so far, as well as decision makers like politicians and business owners, who need clear and strong messages and strong imagery to bring the ocean onto their agenda.

I hope I can do this on the one hand by creating regular and strong content for the (for the moment Instagram-only) platform “Roots to Reefs” to support other young people find their way into ocean conservation, and by helping projects such as the 'Across Maldives' dive expedition to get as much media and political attention as possible and inspire regulatory and behavioural changes.
column23: I hope to become more proficient at storytelling, professional in handling underwater filming equipment and choosing proper settings, so that I can translate what I see underwater into clear, emotional and memorable narratives. I also hope to learn how to plan a shoot most effectively, work with light, and professionalise my post-production skills.
column24:
I am an incredibly motivated and determined person and I love learning. Once I set my mind to something, I put in all the energy and time needed to get to where I need to be, which for me is helping to bring the ocean into the hearts and minds and onto the agendas of as many people as possible, and make it more mainstream, getting the important messages outside of the bubble of ocean lovers, marine scientists and scuba divers. column21:
The underwater world has always fascinated me, from massive coral formations to harmonic schools of fish and encounters with megafauna. I believe underwater filming is a powerful way to bring these experiences to people who may never witness them firsthand.

A defining moment that reshaped how I approach underwater photography occurred when I photographed and filmed the complete mortality of coral reefs near my university. In the winter of 2024, we out-planted corals onto MARRS Reef Stars with optimism. By the following summer, extreme heat stress had killed those corals along with much of the surrounding reef. Returning to document reefs I once photographed as vibrant and flourishing was devastating, and it strengthened my commitment to documenting restoration, not only to support recovery, but to record what is at stake.

In the summer of 2025, witnessing and filming coral spawning during fieldwork restored a sense of hope: a new life cycle beginning and a reminder that resilience, though fragile, remains possible. I created a one-minute video using that spawning footage and shared it during the United Nations Ocean Conference in Nice 2025. The response came mainly from other early-career ocean professionals, which showed me the power of sharing these stories, but the process also revealed my own hesitancy to fully put myself out there creatively, particularly through voice and narrative presence.

I am drawn specifically to Behind the Mask Academy because its values closely align with mine. BTM’s emphasis on observing, listening, documenting with transparency, and telling stories that are both respectful and engaging reflects how I aspire to work as a filmmaker. While I am proud of what I have achieved independently, I believe that structured learning, mentorship, and feedback within a community like BTM are what I need to grow to the next level.
column22:
My vision is to become a hybrid conservation storyteller, combining scientific understanding with cinematic storytelling. I aim to bring the Red Sea to global audiences, not only showcasing its extraordinary beauty, but also highlighting the restoration and conservation efforts working to preserve it.
I want to produce films that support science communication, advocacy, and long-term monitoring by returning to the same reefs over time and documenting change, recovery, and resilience. Through this work, I hope to create emotionally compelling, scientifically grounded stories that help audiences and stakeholders better understand what is being protected and why it matters.
column23:
Through watching BTM tutorials, I have already learned skills that noticeably improved my filming. Through this internship, I hope to refine those skills through structured professional training, hands-on experience,  continuous feedback and fruitful friendly discussions.
For me, success from this internship looks like becoming a scientist who produces wildlife documentaries that are both cinematic and scientifically sound. 
I am particularly interested in developing my ability to work with ambient light in challenging conditions, refine my technical decision-making underwater and topside, and improve color grading and editing for emotional impact. Most importantly, I want to strengthen my ability to translate scientific work into stories that are accurate and engaging without compromising integrity.
column24:
Over the past four years, I have developed strong technical skills in underwater photography, filming, and post-production. Alongside this, I have been a lead diver in my lab for the past three years, responsible for dive planning, supervising daily operations, and ensuring long-term restoration objectives are met safely and efficiently.
This role has strengthened my adaptability, communication, and decision-making under variable environmental conditions, particularly while leading multinational teams with diverse technical backgrounds. With around 400 dives, I have developed strong buoyancy control, situational awareness, and problem-solving skills underwater which are all essential for effective filmmaking in dynamic environments.
My work in coral restoration has also required extensive logistical planning, including budgeting, procurement, material fabrication, storage, and training personnel. These experiences have made me resourceful, organized, and comfortable working in complex, real-world conditions.
I see this internship as a step toward becoming a scientist who produces wildlife documentaries that are cinematic and scientifically rigorous  and I believe my background in research, diving leadership, and visual storytelling, positions me well to contribute meaningfully to the BTM Academy while continuing to grow within it in this internship.

column21:
I used to be terrified of the ocean, but ever since I was a child I knew that we fear the unknown. I educated myself through documentaries and many books and over the years my fear disappeared. Getting over my fear of the ocean made me see the beauty in it; making me want to protect it. 
The more I learned, the more I realised that my fears mostly came from how the media portrayed the ocean; many representations are inaccurate and dramatised to create buzz and get attention for headlines. 
This creates a general fear of the ocean which delays efforts for ocean conservation - understandably so: why protect something that you are scared of? This further disconnects today's society and the sea. 

This is why I got into underwater filming - I want to help guide others to take the same journey as I did in the hope that it will help conservation efforts and bridge the gap between us and the ocean. 

I'm mostly self taught so I had to find reliable and professional sources to learn from: that is how I found Behind the mask's Youtube channel 4 years ago. I very often come back to this channel to refresh my skills and find inspiration for shots: which is why I am applying to this internship with you.
column22:
I grew up watching "The undersea world journal of Jacques Cousteau", and I want to be able to recreate the wonder and magic that I felt in these moments. This series that Cousteau made was my version of Disney movies, they were my fairy tales. 
They show how incredible the ocean is and made my heart pound out of my chest. 

My dream in underwater film making is to be able to put to life a project that I hold very dear: a series of tales to rewrite the narrative that the oceans have been put through over the years by the media. 
I want to be able to create fairy tales out of the seas.

column21:
I am more and more drawn to continue in ocean storytelling and media; this feels like a life path for me, and getting an internship in this environment would be a huge boost in both knowledge and networking. I am also increasingly interested in moving to islands like the Azores, Madeira, or the Canary Islands, as my work is getting more connected to dive centers, sailing, scientific expeditions. And the opportunity to tell stories about these wonderful places is perfectly fitting my future path. 

A friend of mine sent me your website first saying this fits you perfectly! And I can confirm this! I want to deepen my craft in underwater storytelling and surround myself with people who live and work in the ocean media world every day. The academy sits exactly where I want to be: between filmmaking, marine environments, and a community of professionals who can challenge and guide me. Joining this context would accelerate my learning curve and open doors that are hard to reach on my own from a purely freelance and academic path.

I started getting drawn into underwater filming while learning to dive and experimenting with a GoPro in places like the Azores and the Andalusian coast; being under the surface made me realize how many stories are invisible from land. Over time, my documentary work and my time on expeditions pushed me toward the idea that my future is in creating ocean stories that connect communities, science, and culture. I chose BTM Academy because it offers structured, hands-on training with people who already work in this field, and because it feels like the right bridge between my current experience and a more professional, long-term career in ocean media.
column22:
I want to gain these skills first out of a very personal interest, and then put them at the service of a wider, global audience. When I see strong underwater and ocean images, I feel how powerful they are: how much they communicate without a single word, just by looking.  Inspiring younger and older generations through ocean media has become a personal mission for me, after my experiences at sea, working on board as a documenter and doing science communication. I can see how important this work is for the future of the world and of people.

A turning point for me was my first trip on the traditional lugger Grayhound from Pasaia in northern Spain to Gloucester in 2024. That voyage created a “before and after” in my life: it put my life on land into perspective and opened a completely new world for me. Communicating this change, and how positively it affected my mental health and sense of purpose, is a fundamental part of my ocean media projects. I want to show nature and the sea not only as something to protect, but as a powerful tool for personal well-being and connection.

My ultimate vision is to become a filmmaker who uses underwater images to connect people emotionally with the ocean, its cultures, and its hidden stories. I want to live close to the sea, ideally on islands like the Azores, Madeira, or the Canary Islands, and build long-term projects that document both marine life and the communities that live with it.  

Over time, I imagine a body of work made of documentaries, short films, and educational pieces that travel internationally, used in schools, museums, and on board sailing ships. Through these projects, I want to promote ocean literacy, mental well-being through contact with nature, and a sense of global responsibility towards the sea.
column23:
I hope to gain three things from this internship.  

First, I want to solidify my technical and narrative skills in underwater and ocean-focused storytelling, working alongside people who already do this every day and learning their workflows, safety culture, and creative processes. 

Second, I want to understand how a professional ocean media project is built from the inside: from concept to logistics, fieldwork, post-production, and distribution, so I can better structure my own long-term projects on islands and coastal communities.

Third, I am looking to build real relationships and a network in this field, connecting with mentors and collaborators who share the same mission of using ocean media to inspire and educate people of different generations around the world.

So in a sense technical and soft skills.
column24:
I think I am a good candidate because my path has already been moving steadily into ocean storytelling, and the timing is right after several formative sea and media experiences. I have a solid base in documentary filmmaking, from camera work and sound to editing and storytelling, and I am now specializing more and more in ocean-related projects and kickstarting underwater images.

I am used to working in challenging conditions at sea, adapting quickly, and taking responsibility for both technical gear and the people I am filming with. My experiences on sailing ships, expeditions, and coastal communities have given me maturity, teamwork skills, and a calm attitude under pressure.

At the same time, I bring genuine curiosity, empathy with the people I film, and a long-term commitment to using ocean media to inspire others, not just as a short adventure. I am ready to learn, to contribute creatively and practically, and to fully dedicate this phase of my life to growing inside this field.

My goal has been to use photography as a way to help people connect with marine life and the ocean. Now I’m hoping to expand that work into underwater filmmaking and learn how to tell these stories through motion and film.
column17:
Over the past few years most of my time has been focused on ocean photography, freediving, and spending as much time in the water as possible documenting marine life. I grew up sailing and later worked as a sailing coach, which gave me a strong foundation being in and around the ocean from a young age. I’m also a Wave 3 freediver and a PADI Rescue Diver, which has helped me work safely in the water while photographing wildlife.

Along the way I’ve supported myself through ocean related work including ecotourism, guiding, and expedition opportunities. Those experiences have given me a lot of time in the field around marine wildlife while continuing to build my photography portfolio.

In the past few years I’ve focused on growing as an ocean photographer, building my portfolio, and sharing my work with a wider audience. My goal has always been to create images that help people feel connected to the ocean and the animals that live there.

While photography has been the foundation of my work, I’m really interested in expanding into film. I see underwater filmmaking as the next step for turning my perspective as a photographer into deeper stories about the ocean and the wildlife I spend time documenting.
column18: Most of my experience so far has been in underwater photography rather than filmmaking. I’ve spent a lot of time in the water photographing marine wildlife while freediving and working around animals like whales, dolphins, sharks, and rays. I’ve only just started getting into video recently and have barely begun learning DaVinci Resolve, so I’m still very new to the filmmaking side of things. That’s a big part of why this opportunity interests me. I’d love to learn how to turn the way I already see and photograph the ocean into stronger storytelling through film.

column21:
I’ve spent the last several years focused on ocean photography, documenting marine wildlife and building a body of work around the ocean. Photography has been the foundation of how I share these experiences, but lately I’ve been feeling a pull to take my work beyond still images and social media and start telling deeper stories through film.

When I was little I was completely obsessed with ocean documentaries like National Geographic and BBC Blue Planet (still am). I remember watching them and thinking that one day I wanted to be the person behind the camera filming those moments. That feeling has never really gone away.

I’ve realized that if I want to move in that direction, it’s time for me to actually learn filmmaking. I have a lot of experience with photography and feel very comfortable working in the water with a camera, but video still feels like a completely different world to me. That’s a big reason why a mentorship like this is so appealing.

BTM Academy stood out to me because it seems very focused on real experience in the ocean and learning alongside people who are actively working in underwater filmmaking. Being able to develop those skills in an environment centered around ocean storytelling would be an incredible next step for me.
column22:
My long term goal is to create work that helps people feel connected to the ocean and the animals that live there. Through photography I’ve seen how powerful imagery can be in making people care about places and species they may never experience themselves, and I think film has the potential to take that even further.

I know I’m still at the beginning of the filmmaking side of things, but an ultimate dream of mine would be to work on real ocean documentaries, the kind of films I grew up watching on National Geographic or BBC’s Blue Planet. Being part of a team documenting wildlife and telling meaningful stories about the ocean would be incredible.

For now my focus is on learning the craft of underwater filmmaking and building the skills needed to tell those kinds of stories. Photography has been my foundation, and I see filmmaking as the next step in growing as a storyteller in the ocean.
column23:
Right now I feel very comfortable working in the ocean with a camera through photography, but filmmaking is still really new for me. One of the main things I’d love to gain from this internship is learning the technical side of underwater videography. Things like camera settings for video, filming techniques underwater, color grading, and how to actually edit footage together.

I’m also really interested in learning how to build a story through film. With photography I’m used to capturing individual moments, but film is a completely different way of thinking and I’d love to understand how to turn footage into something that actually tells a story.

And if there’s any chance to learn a bit about the professional side of underwater filmmaking too, like how people turn this kind of work into a career or collaborate on bigger projects, that would be incredibly valuable.
column24:
I think I’d be a good candidate because I’m at a point in my life where I’m fully committed to pursuing this path. I’m 23 and all in on building a career around the ocean and visual storytelling.

Over the past few years I’ve worked really hard to grow my photography portfolio, build an audience of over 400k across platforms, and support myself through my work in ocean photography. That process has taken a lot of persistence and has made me even more motivated to keep pushing forward.

I’m eager to keep learning and excited to start taking the next step in my career. My schedule is flexible and I’m ready to fully commit the time and energy needed if selected.

column21:
I’ve been following your work for the past two years, and since the very first day your company was recommended to me, I’ve recognized your vision and quality. I share the same love for the ocean and marine life.
Since I was very young, I’ve had a deep passion for the aquatic environment, and although I swam professionally until I was 17, I believe the skills I developed now allow me to pursue new opportunities, such as becoming part of your team with both quality and genuine enthusiasm.
I’ve always taken photographs, but at 15 I decided to share my vision with the world.

I believe your work not only inspires people to discover the ocean, but also plays an important role in promoting respect for it, something I strive to reflect in my own work as well.
column22:
What I’ve always sought is to tell stories, to create imaginary worlds in my mind and bring them to life. I believe there is great importance in imagination, and although my success has come from documentary photography, something less imaginary and more “real”, the ocean holds that ancestral quality of being something we can visualize without ever having a fixed “setting.”Its very existence offers the freedom to see beyond what is in front of us. Even as we grow familiar with it, we still feel the need to frame it within a greater meaning, to give it purpose. 

In a way, I find myself crossing into this universe with the belief that I can be part of its story, by documenting it, respecting it, and helping to share it with others, creating a cycle of awareness that both the ocean and we, as people, deeply need.
column23: Good friendships, meaningful time spent in the ocean, and of course, great footage, this is what I believe to be the beginning of another journey.

column21:
I have worked directly with BTM, and I was deeply inspired by them. They were living my dream—being able to dive and document the underwater world from their own perspective.
The BTM Academy is a great opportunity for me to expand my knowledge, and I believe it would be great to bring my own style and artistic approach to the team. I want to learn, but also to contribute with different perspectives, grow as a creator, and go beyond my current limits. My ambitions are high, and that’s why I believe the BTM Academy is my next big step.
column22:
As a freediver, you always want to go further and beyond your limits—that’s what I feel about underwater photography. I’ve been lucky enough to live in Portugal and to have a strong connection with the Azores. I’ve seen many beautiful things, and sometimes I feel a bit lonely because most people can’t truly feel what I feel underwater.
With photography, my ambition isn’t just to take the best picture in the underwater photography world. I want to go deeper and bring the ocean to people who don’t even dive—to show a world that only a few can reach, experience, and truly connect with.
I would love to work with film photography underwater. It’s challenging and quite rare, but analog photography has been my passion since I was 17, when I started documenting teenage life. Now that I’m older and have developed a deep love for the ocean, I want to combine freediving with analog photography—to tell stories that only images can tell.
column23:
I believe this internship would be a great opportunity not only to develop my photography skills but also to gain a deeper understanding of this world. I want to learn more about the business side of underwater photography and explore new perspectives and ideas.

It’s not just about taking pictures underwater—it’s much more than that. It’s about real-world connections and goes far beyond social media. Anyone can be an underwater influencer, but only a few can become truly artistic directors and bring human creativity into the beautiful world of ocean life.
column24:
As I mentioned before, I want to learn everything I can, but I also want to bring new perspectives to the table. I want to grow personally while feeling that I am making a real difference.
I’m young, but I have already had the opportunity to work directly with BTM, and I believe I can be part of the team. Over the past four years, I have learned a lot about boats, the diving industry, and what people are truly looking for when they call themselves divers.
It’s impossible not to connect with the ocean; Some people just take a little more time. I want to bring the ocean to those who don’t dive. I want to share the feeling of peace I experience underwater with someone sitting next to me who has never dived before.
That, to me, is the most meaningful thing I can do as an artist—not just to observe art, but to become part of it. To connect deeply with nature and bring that connection outward, expressing what I believe is part of the true purpose of life.

column14: I am currently completing my Master’s thesis internship at the OKEANOS Institute of Marine Sciences (University of the Azores), based in Faial, where I study the population structure of whale sharks.
column16: I am doing studies in marine ecology and I am currently finishing my Master’s degree in Biodiversity, Biology and Ecology, with a specialization in Tropical, Aquatic, Coastal and Island ecosystems.
column17: Over the past few years, I have mainly focused on my studies, while dedicating my free time to diving and hiking. During my Master’s, I lived on Reunion Island, where I regularly went scuba diving, snorkeling, and freediving with friends on weekends and holidays. I also spent time exploring the island by hiking and camping in the mountains.
column18:
Through my studies, I have gained experience in underwater filming, mainly for scientific data collection purposes. For example, I filmed whale sharks while freediving to later conduct photo-identification analyses, and recorded coral reef footage while snorkeling to assess coral cover at different sites.
I also completed a professional scientific diving certification (CAH1B), during which I was trained to capture video data and to use underwater lighting effectively to improve image quality.
Beyond academic work, I regularly film during my dives, snorkeling, or freediving.

column21:
I have always been drawn to underwater videos, whether showing marine animals, habitats, or scenery. Every time I am in the water, I am amazed by what I see, and I want to be able to capture these moments. I am particularly motivated to improve my technical skills, both in filming and post-production, to create quality footage.
Beyond the visual aspect, I see underwater filmmaking as a powerful tool for  outreach. Sharing what I have the opportunity to witness underwater is very important to me, and I believe it can raise awareness and encourage people to better understand and protect marine ecosystems.

When I met Dada and she explained what she was doing, I was amazed because it was similar to the job I had dreamed of when I was little. I was also already familiar with some of Behind the Mask’s work that I had seen on Instagram while researching the Azores, which reinforced my interest in your projects.
Although I chose to pursue a path in marine science, this opportunity feels like the chance to step into the field of underwater filmmaking and connect my studies to my creative side. 
I am motivated by the opportunity to contribute to sharing the underwater world with people who don’t necessarily have access to it and have little knowledge of it. In particular, I would like to help change the negative perception of sharks by showing them in their natural environment, in situations where they are not portrayed as evil, and by highlighting their ecological importance, which I believe can support their conservation.
column22:
I have an academic background focused on marine research and conservation, and I am looking to learn new skills and explore new ways of engaging with the underwater world.
My long-term goal is to build a career that contributes to the conservation of tropical marine ecosystems through both scientific research and outreach. I would like to connect these two fields by integrating underwater filmmaking into my work, for example, by documenting research expeditions, daily fieldwork, and the realities of working in marine science.
Through this, I would like to make science more accessible and share the beauty, fragility, and importance of marine ecosystems, to encourage people to engage in marine protection.
column23:
I believe this internship would allow me to gain valuable hands-on experience and develop practical skills in underwater filmmaking. It would also be the opportunity to better understand the different parameters that influence underwater footage, and learn how to use them effectively to produce high-quality images within a production project.
I also see this as a unique opportunity to learn from professionals with diverse backgrounds and experiences, but with a common interest in the underwater world. Building connections in this field would also be valuable for my future career.

Overall, this internship would help me become more polyvalent by combining my scientific background with creative and technical skills, and it would be an important step toward achieving my goals.
column24:

In addition, I have specific knowledge of the Azores, particularly regarding whale shark populations, which could be valuable in a local context.
My diving certifications and my freediving experiences, have made me very comfortable in the water. This allows me to stay focused on filming while remaining aware of my surroundings. 
I am also really motivated to learn, to be involved in real productions, and to contribute to your projects.

column21:
As a filmmaker and journalist, I’ve learned that storytelling is one of the most powerful tools we have to connect people to places, ideas, and information. My journey as a journalist began in the ocean. I remember being underwater for the first time when all of a sudden it all clicked for me. In that moment, I realized the immense privilege of experiencing a world so few ever get to see and how this incredible world needed to be seen, appreciated and protected. And from acknowledging that privilege, I’ve brought my passion to shine a light on the ocean and the people who depend on it. 

To so many, the ocean is a distant, almost otherworldly place, a place that hides its most fascinating stories beneath. The ocean is our nourishment, protection, livelihood, and the air we breathe.
column22:
My vision is to direct short and feature documentaries and work with outlets like PBS Nature, the BBC Natural History Unit, National Geographic, The New York Times, etc,  or emerging platforms where I can pitch, film, and produce stories about the intersection of people and the natural world. I also want to build a career as a freelance underwater cinematographer and photographer, with a particular focus on elevating stories from Mexico and Latin America and supporting Spanish-speaking filmmakers trying to find their footing in this space.

At the heart of it all is the ocean, the animals that inhabit it and the communities that depend on it. My goal is to produce incredibly rich work that communicates the urgent need for ocean conservation and everyone connected to it.The underwater world gave me my sense of purpose as a storyteller. This program would help me build the skills to fully pursue it.
column23: I would love to see how professionals work in the industry, be mentored, see what works, what doesn't, ask questions and learn about this field as much as possible. I think being out in the field is the best place to learn and its rare that opportunities like these exists. I want to be able to operate a camera underwater, assist if possible and get better at filming underwater. I want to leave with a much stronger foundation behind the underwater camera and a clearer sense of the professional standards this field demands. I also hope to be mentored. I'm at a stage in my career where having access to people who know how the industry work is the best way to make a career for myself and collaborate along the way.
column24: I think I am a good candidate for this internship because I am a storyteller and filmmaker above all. I've been telling stories for the past seven years, but I want to be able to film them underwater. I know how to identify a good story, how to build trust with people, and how to think about images with intention. I'm now at the point in my career where I want to bring all of that into the underwater space, and I'm really committed to putting in the work to get there. I am still willing to learn how to tell stories, and I want to learn from people and connect with others in the same field. I think this is an incredible opportunity and it would be incredible to have the chance to be a part of it.
